hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Checking In, Speaking Up -- Hotels, Reservations, and Handling Problems Confidently
lesson.menu_desc: The front desk of a New York hotel: your reservation is wrong, the Wi-Fi code fails, and the checkout time is off -- and you fix all three without raising your voice. The words of the desk (to apologize, inconvenience, to accommodate, courtesy, to resolve, discrepancy, to escalate, reasonable) and the grammar that makes you sound firm but polite: modal verbs for polite requests and advice -- 'Could you upgrade my room?', 'Would it be possible to check in early?', 'You should speak to the manager.' -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 17 -- Checking In, Speaking Up
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 17 -- Checking In, Speaking Up
lesson.grammar_point: modal verbs for polite requests and advice

listenings.text: Good evening, and thank you for your patience at the front desk tonight. This is a message for our arriving guests. We are running a little behind on housekeeping, so a few rooms are not ready yet, and we want to be honest with you about that. If your room is not ready when you check in, please come and see us, and we will hold your bags and offer you a drink in the lounge while you wait. That is the least we can do. Now, a quick word about reservations. If you booked a specific room type, a superior room, an ocean view, a quiet floor, please check your confirmation when you arrive, because now and then the system assigns the wrong category. If you notice a discrepancy, tell us right away. We would much rather fix it at check-in than after you have carried your suitcase upstairs. And if anything at all goes wrong during your stay, a code that does not work, a charge you do not recognize, a checkout time that looks off, do not sit and worry about it. Come to the desk, ask for me by name, and we will resolve it. A good hotel is not one where nothing ever goes wrong. It is one where every problem gets fixed quickly, and with a smile.

Hello, this is a message for Ms. Ito in room fourteen-oh-two. This is Sarah, the duty manager, and I am calling to follow up on the problem you reported at the front desk this afternoon. First, I want to apologize for the inconvenience. You booked a superior room with an ocean view, and we gave you a standard room on the ground floor, and that is our mistake, not yours. So here is what we have done. We have moved you to a superior room on the ninth floor, which does have the ocean view you asked for, and your key cards are ready at the desk whenever you are. As a courtesy, and because you were so patient, we have added a complimentary breakfast for both mornings, and we have removed the minibar charge that you did not recognize. That was the discrepancy on your bill, and you were right to point it out. I think that is a reasonable way to make things right, but if you feel it is not enough, please ask for me directly and I will look at it again. Thank you for letting us fix this, Ms. Ito. Enjoy the rest of your stay in New York.

items.situation: You arrive and your room is not ready yet. Ask the front desk to hold your bags -- politely.
tips: Could you hold my bags for an hour while I wait? Thank you.
'Could you...?' is the soft, polite request -- warmer and more natural than 'Can you...?' with a stranger who is helping you.

items.situation: Your confirmation says superior room, but they gave you a standard. Point out the discrepancy and ask them to fix it.
tips: There seems to be a discrepancy -- I booked a superior room, so would it be possible to move me?
'There seems to be a discrepancy' is firm but not aggressive -- you name the problem without blaming the person in front of you.

items.situation: The Wi-Fi code on your card does not work. Report it and ask them to resolve it.
tips: The Wi-Fi code isn't working -- could someone help me resolve it?
You report the fact, then make a polite request with 'could' -- that combination gets things done at any front desk.

items.situation: A friend at the hotel is unhappy with their room but too shy to say anything. Give them advice.
tips: You should speak to the front desk -- they can't fix a problem they don't know about.
'You should...' is the natural way to give advice -- no 'to' after it: 'you should speak', never 'you should to speak'.

items.situation: There is a charge on your bill you do not recognize. Ask about it without accusing anyone.
tips: I'm sorry, there's a charge here I don't recognize -- would you mind checking it for me?
'Would you mind checking...?' keeps it light -- you assume it's an honest mistake, which makes staff want to help.

items.situation: The front desk cannot help and you need a decision from someone higher up. Ask to escalate -- politely.
tips: I understand it's not your decision -- could I speak with the duty manager, please?
You escalate without attacking the person: acknowledge their limit ('it's not your decision'), then ask calmly for the manager.
